page: Amazon Web Services m8in.24xlarge is a general-purpose, network and EBS-optimized virtual machine instance. Powered by the AWS Nitro hypervisor, this instance features a dedicated Intel x86_64 processor architecture with 96 virtual central processing units derived from 48 physical cores. The processor operates at a clock speed of 3.9 GHz, supporting two threads per core to handle concurrent multi-threaded execution.

The instance is configured with 384.0 GB of system memory, maintaining a balanced ratio of 4.0 GB of RAM per physical core. It does not include local instance storage, relying entirely on network-attached Elastic Block Store volumes for persistent storage operations. Networking capabilities are a key feature of this instance, which delivers a baseline network bandwidth of 150 Gbps to support high-throughput data transfers.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the m8in.24xlarge offers a high-density resource profile suited for enterprise-scale workloads that require substantial network throughput and memory capacity without the need for local NVMe drives or GPU acceleration. The combination of high-frequency Intel processors and 150 Gbps network bandwidth makes this instance suitable for multi-tier enterprise applications, network-intensive middle-tier services, database caching layers, and large-scale application servers.
description: Amazon Web Services m8in.24xlarge is an x86_64 general-purpose instance optimized for network and EBS performance. Running on the AWS Nitro hypervisor, it features 96 vCPUs across 48 dedicated physical Intel cores operating at 3.9 GHz. The system is equipped with 384.0 GB of RAM, providing a balanced 4.0 GB of memory per core. This instance does not include local storage, relying instead on network-attached storage solutions. It provides a high baseline network bandwidth of 150 Gbps, making it highly efficient for data-intensive network transfers. The m8in.24xlarge is designed for enterprise workloads requiring high network throughput and balanced compute-to-memory resources, such as large-scale application servers, database caching layers, and network-intensive middle-tier applications.
